blocks.content: The evening wound toward its end in the way good evenings do — slowly, without anyone wanting to be the first to say it was time to go.

They had moved back into a looser arrangement: some standing, some seated, the table between them holding the empty cups and the trivia cards and the small debris of a night that had been full. The record player had cycled through to something lighter, and no one had changed it.
It was Lena who noticed the carnival poster in its second context — she had walked past the window to get her coat from the rack, and the lamp post outside was catching the edge of it.

blocks.speaker: Lena
blocks.content: The carnival's this weekend. I keep forgetting about it.

blocks.speaker: Nathan
blocks.content: Friday through Sunday.

blocks.speaker: Lena
blocks.content: I used to love those as a kid. There was one every summer where I grew up. Same rides every year, same smells. There's something about them.
She looked at Cassandra.

blocks.speaker: Lena
blocks.content: Remember the one we went to four years ago?

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: You cried on the Ferris wheel.

blocks.speaker: Lena
blocks.content: I was moved by the view.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: You were crying.

blocks.speaker: Lena
blocks.content: Emotionally moved. It's different.
A small laugh went around the table.
Cassandra was looking at the poster through the window. Then she turned to Ethan.
She didn't say it lightly. The quality of it — the way she turned, the way she looked at him, the specific weight before the words — all of it made clear this was not casual.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Come with me tomorrow. To the carnival.
He looked at her.

He understood, somehow, that this was not only an invitation to a carnival. It was an invitation to whatever came after the carnival. To the thing she had said she would tell him. To the whole of her — the version that existed with him and the version that had been kept separate.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Yes.
Simply. Without hesitation.
Something in her expression settled — the particular settling of a person who has said the thing and had it received.

Around them, the others continued their quiet conversations. The record played. The candle at the front table had burned down to a low steady flame. Outside, the carnival poster moved slightly in the night air.
Tomorrow. The fair. The Ferris wheel and the lights and the specific kind of joy that is both childish and necessary.
And after that — the truth.
Cassandra looked at Ethan one more time before people started finding their coats.
He was already looking at her.
